item.title: Ep 1 · Ch 1 — Separating Clinical Technique From Its Underlying Metaphysics
item.description:
Chapter 1: The Integration of Psychology and Christianity
How to critically evaluate a therapy model by asking what it assumes about human nature and flourishing, then keeping the working mechanism (tracking a cycle, understanding trauma's effect on the nervous system) while discarding the philosophical baggage. Covers the two failure modes of summary dismissal and uncritical embrace, and why the siloed "levels of explanation" approach leaves faith and practice never informing each other.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 1: The Integration of Psychology and Christianity
How to critically evaluate a therapy model by asking what it assumes about human nature and flourishing, then keeping the working mechanism (tracking a cycle, understanding trauma's effect on the nervous system) while discarding the philosophical baggage. Covers the two failure modes of summary dismissal and uncritical embrace, and why the siloed "levels of explanation" approach leaves faith and practice never informing each other.

item.title: Ep 2 · Ch 1 — When Doctrinal Anxiety Blocks Attunement in Session
item.description:
Chapter 1: The Integration of Psychology and Christianity
What happens in the room when a therapist's defensive posture toward secular psychology turns them into a debater or fixer instead of a safe haven — rushing to correct a client's theology rather than holding their pain. Listeners learn to monitor the urge to correct, distinguish protecting their own anxiety from tending the client's attachment need, and ground their faith identity before session so they don't have to defend it during one.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 1: The Integration of Psychology and Christianity
What happens in the room when a therapist's defensive posture toward secular psychology turns them into a debater or fixer instead of a safe haven — rushing to correct a client's theology rather than holding their pain. Listeners learn to monitor the urge to correct, distinguish protecting their own anxiety from tending the client's attachment need, and ground their faith identity before session so they don't have to defend it during one.

item.title: Ep 3 · Ch 2 — Imago Dei as an Anchor Against Client Shame
item.description:
Chapter 2: A Christian View of Persons
How the doctrine of the Imago Dei gives the therapist a stable conviction of the client's inherent worth when the client insists they are broken, toxic, or beyond repair. Covers holding the tension between deep woundedness and indestructible core value, and how to reflect back even limited agency in traumatized clients.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 2: A Christian View of Persons
How the doctrine of the Imago Dei gives the therapist a stable conviction of the client's inherent worth when the client insists they are broken, toxic, or beyond repair. Covers holding the tension between deep woundedness and indestructible core value, and how to reflect back even limited agency in traumatized clients.

item.title: Ep 4 · Ch 2 — Treating the Embodied Soul Across Four Dimensions
item.description:
Chapter 2: A Christian View of Persons
A working playbook for the biopsychosocial-spiritual model: assessing the physical baseline, mapping the relational cycle, tracking emotion, and exploring the client's meaning-making framework. Includes the move of pivoting between dimensions to unblock a stuck client, and the red flags of spiritualizing a biological problem or medicalizing a spiritual one.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 2: A Christian View of Persons
A working playbook for the biopsychosocial-spiritual model: assessing the physical baseline, mapping the relational cycle, tracking emotion, and exploring the client's meaning-making framework. Includes the move of pivoting between dimensions to unblock a stuck client, and the red flags of spiritualizing a biological problem or medicalizing a spiritual one.

item.title: Ep 5 · Ch 3 — Where Id Was: Reading the Dynamic Unconscious
item.description:
Chapter 3: Classical Psychoanalysis
Freud's structural model (id, ego, superego) and the dynamic unconscious explain why a client's rage over a dish in the sink is never about the dish. Learn to treat a reaction disproportionate to its trigger as your cue that unconscious material is live, then shift from content to the fear underneath.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 3: Classical Psychoanalysis
Freud's structural model (id, ego, superego) and the dynamic unconscious explain why a client's rage over a dish in the sink is never about the dish. Learn to treat a reaction disproportionate to its trigger as your cue that unconscious material is live, then shift from content to the fear underneath.

item.title: Ep 6 · Ch 3 — Letting Transference Build: Resistance Is the Real Work
item.description:
Chapter 3: Classical Psychoanalysis
How to use the therapeutic relationship itself as the vehicle for change: hold a non-reactive stance so the client's relational template lands on you, then interpret the resistance instead of defending yourself. Includes why premature interpretation hardens defenses and how to pace the link back to the historical wound.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 3: Classical Psychoanalysis
How to use the therapeutic relationship itself as the vehicle for change: hold a non-reactive stance so the client's relational template lands on you, then interpret the resistance instead of defending yourself. Includes why premature interpretation hardens defenses and how to pace the link back to the historical wound.

item.title: Ep 7 · Ch 3 — Respect the Armor: Working With Defenses, Not Against
item.description:
Chapter 3: Classical Psychoanalysis
When denial, projection, or intellectualization stall the work, the answer is to lower the anxiety driving the defense rather than strip the defense away. Learn to name a defense's protective function out loud, track anxiety spikes in session, and avoid the confrontation that makes a guarded client double down.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 3: Classical Psychoanalysis
When denial, projection, or intellectualization stall the work, the answer is to lower the anxiety driving the defense rather than strip the defense away. Learn to name a defense's protective function out loud, track anxiety spikes in session, and avoid the confrontation that makes a guarded client double down.

item.title: Ep 8 · Ch 3 — The Untuned Instrument: Countertransference as Diagnostic Data
item.description:
Chapter 3: Classical Psychoanalysis
Your dread, irritation, or urge to placate a difficult client is information about the client's relational pressure, not a professional failure. Learn to catch countertransference in the body before the mind, bring what the client makes you feel into supervision, and separate the historical ghost from the person in the room.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 3: Classical Psychoanalysis
Your dread, irritation, or urge to placate a difficult client is information about the client's relational pressure, not a professional failure. Learn to catch countertransference in the body before the mind, bring what the client makes you feel into supervision, and separate the historical ghost from the person in the room.

item.title: Ep 9 · Ch 4 — Internalized Objects: Arguing With a Ghost, Not Your Partner
item.description:
Chapter 4: Contemporary Psychodynamic Psychotherapies
Object relations and self psychology recast pathology as a distorted internal map of relationships rather than blocked biological drives, so an outsized reaction to a partner's sigh is really a collision with an internalized caregiver. Listeners learn to stop reality-testing, explore what the trigger meant about the client's self, and use consistent mirroring to build a cohesive self.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 4: Contemporary Psychodynamic Psychotherapies
Object relations and self psychology recast pathology as a distorted internal map of relationships rather than blocked biological drives, so an outsized reaction to a partner's sigh is really a collision with an internalized caregiver. Listeners learn to stop reality-testing, explore what the trigger meant about the client's self, and use consistent mirroring to build a cohesive self.

item.title: Ep 10 · Ch 4 — Mapping the Cyclical Maladaptive Pattern in the Room
item.description:
Chapter 4: Contemporary Psychodynamic Psychotherapies
Time-limited dynamic therapy compresses years of analysis into one focused question: what relational loop does this client repeat everywhere, including with you? Listeners learn to write a client's CMP in a single sentence, feel the pull to play rescuer or judge without enacting it, and name the dynamic out loud instead.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 4: Contemporary Psychodynamic Psychotherapies
Time-limited dynamic therapy compresses years of analysis into one focused question: what relational loop does this client repeat everywhere, including with you? Listeners learn to write a client's CMP in a single sentence, feel the pull to play rescuer or judge without enacting it, and name the dynamic out loud instead.

item.title: Ep 11 · Ch 4 — Splitting: Surviving Idealization, Devaluation and the Holding Environment
item.description:
Chapter 4: Contemporary Psychodynamic Psychotherapies
With borderline and narcissistic dynamics, insight-oriented work destabilizes a self that was never integrated, so the therapist must become the holding environment that contains both the all-good and all-bad object. Listeners learn to recognize the sudden flip from savior to enemy, contain rather than defend, and use the "and" intervention plus a rock-solid frame.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 4: Contemporary Psychodynamic Psychotherapies
With borderline and narcissistic dynamics, insight-oriented work destabilizes a self that was never integrated, so the therapist must become the holding environment that contains both the all-good and all-bad object. Listeners learn to recognize the sudden flip from savior to enemy, contain rather than defend, and use the "and" intervention plus a rock-solid frame.

item.title: Ep 12 · Ch 4 — The Co-Created Third: Immediacy, Enactment and Repair
item.description:
Chapter 4: Contemporary Psychodynamic Psychotherapies
Relational two- and three-person models abandon the blank screen: the boredom, fog or tension in the room is co-created data, not just the client's resistance. Listeners learn disciplined self-disclosure that names the therapist's experience of the relationship, how to spot an enactment they have stepped into, and why the repair, not the rupture, is the intervention.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 4: Contemporary Psychodynamic Psychotherapies
Relational two- and three-person models abandon the blank screen: the boredom, fog or tension in the room is co-created data, not just the client's resistance. Listeners learn disciplined self-disclosure that names the therapist's experience of the relationship, how to spot an enactment they have stepped into, and why the repair, not the rupture, is the intervention.

item.title: Ep 13 · Ch 5 — Reinforcement, Shaping, and the Pursue-Withdraw Cycle
item.description:
Chapter 5: Behavior Therapy
How classical, operant, and social learning principles quietly maintain a couple's escalation-and-shutdown loop, and why the therapist must disrupt the reinforcement contingency rather than mine for insight. Includes identifying the reinforcer and shaping small approximations of vulnerable attachment cues.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 5: Behavior Therapy
How classical, operant, and social learning principles quietly maintain a couple's escalation-and-shutdown loop, and why the therapist must disrupt the reinforcement contingency rather than mine for insight. Includes identifying the reinforcer and shaping small approximations of vulnerable attachment cues.

item.title: Ep 14 · Ch 5 — Extinguishing Conditioned Anxiety Through Graded Exposure
item.description:
Chapter 5: Behavior Therapy
A practical playbook for systematic desensitization: name the conditioned stimulus, map the avoidance, teach a competing relaxation response, and build a fear hierarchy. Learn why stopping an exposure at peak panic reinforces the fear, and how empathy means walking a client into avoidance rather than around it.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 5: Behavior Therapy
A practical playbook for systematic desensitization: name the conditioned stimulus, map the avoidance, teach a competing relaxation response, and build a fear hierarchy. Learn why stopping an exposure at peak panic reinforces the fear, and how empathy means walking a client into avoidance rather than around it.

item.title: Ep 15 · Ch 5 — Response Prevention and the Refusal to Reassure
item.description:
Chapter 5: Behavior Therapy
Marks' 1981 OCD case shows how compulsive washing operantly reinforces the very anxiety it relieves, and how exposure with response prevention collapses the cycle. The listener learns why premature reassurance robs the client of the corrective learning, and what bedrock alliance ERP requires.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 5: Behavior Therapy
Marks' 1981 OCD case shows how compulsive washing operantly reinforces the very anxiety it relieves, and how exposure with response prevention collapses the cycle. The listener learns why premature reassurance robs the client of the corrective learning, and what bedrock alliance ERP requires.

item.title: Ep 16 · Ch 5 — When Couples Weaponize Their Communication Skills
item.description:
Chapter 5: Behavior Therapy
Why behavioral marital therapy relapses once partners turn their new skills into precision instruments for blame, and how integrative behavioral couple therapy answers with acceptance and emotional deepening instead of more coaching. Listeners learn to recognize the wall where skill-building stops working, drop the technique, and surface the vulnerability underneath the behavior each partner calls intolerable.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 5: Behavior Therapy
Why behavioral marital therapy relapses once partners turn their new skills into precision instruments for blame, and how integrative behavioral couple therapy answers with acceptance and emotional deepening instead of more coaching. Listeners learn to recognize the wall where skill-building stops working, drop the technique, and surface the vulnerability underneath the behavior each partner calls intolerable.

item.title: Ep 17 · Ch 5 — Determinism, Agency, and the Sliver of Freedom
item.description:
Chapter 5: Behavior Therapy
How to hold the tension between Skinner's determinism and a Christian anthropology of agency when a client says "I'm just wired this way." Covers validating the logic of past conditioning while locating micro-moments of choice, and avoiding the "just choose joy" spiritual bypass.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 5: Behavior Therapy
How to hold the tension between Skinner's determinism and a Christian anthropology of agency when a client says "I'm just wired this way." Covers validating the logic of past conditioning while locating micro-moments of choice, and avoiding the "just choose joy" spiritual bypass.

item.title: Ep 18 · Ch 6 — The Three Waves: From Restructuring to Acceptance
item.description:
Chapter 6: Cognitive Therapy
Traces cognitive therapy's evolution from behaviorism through Ellis and Beck's thought-challenging to third-wave mindfulness and acceptance, and shows how to pick the right wave for the client in front of you. Listeners learn why rigid second-wave disputation can pile shame on top of anxiety, and how dropping the rope with a painful thought restores self-compassion.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 6: Cognitive Therapy
Traces cognitive therapy's evolution from behaviorism through Ellis and Beck's thought-challenging to third-wave mindfulness and acceptance, and shows how to pick the right wave for the client in front of you. Listeners learn why rigid second-wave disputation can pile shame on top of anxiety, and how dropping the rope with a painful thought restores self-compassion.

item.title: Ep 19 · Ch 6 — Collaborative Empiricism: Questioning Instead of Debating Clients
item.description:
Chapter 6: Cognitive Therapy
Walks through Beck's collaborative empiricism and Socratic questioning as an alternative to adversarially disputing a client's beliefs, including how to catch an automatic thought live from an affect shift. Listeners get concrete moves for weighing evidence with a depressed client without slipping into reassurance or debate.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 6: Cognitive Therapy
Walks through Beck's collaborative empiricism and Socratic questioning as an alternative to adversarially disputing a client's beliefs, including how to catch an automatic thought live from an affect shift. Listeners get concrete moves for weighing evidence with a depressed client without slipping into reassurance or debate.

item.title: Ep 20 · Ch 6 — Rewriting the Story When Disputing Thoughts Fails
item.description:
Chapter 6: Cognitive Therapy
Applies Meichenbaum's constructivist view to clients whose narrative of victimhood or brokenness is too rigidly built for standard cognitive challenging. Listeners learn to validate the historical pain, hunt for the exceptions the client overlooks, and co-author a story of resilience rather than argue the old one down.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 6: Cognitive Therapy
Applies Meichenbaum's constructivist view to clients whose narrative of victimhood or brokenness is too rigidly built for standard cognitive challenging. Listeners learn to validate the historical pain, hunt for the exceptions the client overlooks, and co-author a story of resilience rather than argue the old one down.

item.title: Ep 21 · Ch 6 — When Guilt Is True: Cognitive Therapy's Relativism Problem
item.description:
Chapter 6: Cognitive Therapy
Examines the Christian critique of functional relativism, where "rational" quietly means "whatever makes the client feel better," and the danger of restructuring away a conscience that is working correctly. Listeners learn to distinguish a genuine distortion from an accurate moral appraisal, and to treat the latter with confession and repair rather than cognitive bypass.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 6: Cognitive Therapy
Examines the Christian critique of functional relativism, where "rational" quietly means "whatever makes the client feel better," and the danger of restructuring away a conscience that is working correctly. Listeners learn to distinguish a genuine distortion from an accurate moral appraisal, and to treat the latter with confession and repair rather than cognitive bypass.

item.title: Ep 22 · Ch 7 — Rogers's Triad and the Organismic Valuing Process
item.description:
Chapter 7: Person-Centered Therapy
Unpacks empathy, congruence, and unconditional positive regard as states of being rather than techniques, and shows how conditions of worth sever a client from their own bodily sense of what they want. Demonstrates the pivot from giving advice to pure reflection when a client begs the therapist to become the next external authority.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 7: Person-Centered Therapy
Unpacks empathy, congruence, and unconditional positive regard as states of being rather than techniques, and shows how conditions of worth sever a client from their own bodily sense of what they want. Demonstrates the pivot from giving advice to pure reflection when a client begs the therapist to become the next external authority.

item.title: Ep 23 · Ch 7 — When Pure Reflection Abandons the Client
item.description:
Chapter 7: Person-Centered Therapy
Maps where nondirective therapy fails — severe trauma, addiction, escalated couples — and why empathy is necessary but no longer sufficient. Teaches the shift from reflection to active structuring, including how to interrupt verbal abuse in session without abandoning Rogerian respect.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 7: Person-Centered Therapy
Maps where nondirective therapy fails — severe trauma, addiction, escalated couples — and why empathy is necessary but no longer sufficient. Teaches the shift from reflection to active structuring, including how to interrupt verbal abuse in session without abandoning Rogerian respect.

item.title: Ep 24 · Ch 7 — Authentic Self Versus Covenant: Christian Critiques of Self-Actualization
item.description:
Chapter 7: Person-Centered Therapy
Examines the tension between Rogers's ethical egoism and a sacred humanism grounded in the imago dei and community obligation, using a husband who weaponizes 'being true to myself' to justify leaving his family. Shows how to prize the person unconditionally while refusing to bless every choice.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 7: Person-Centered Therapy
Examines the tension between Rogers's ethical egoism and a sacred humanism grounded in the imago dei and community obligation, using a husband who weaponizes 'being true to myself' to justify leaving his family. Shows how to prize the person unconditionally while refusing to bless every choice.

item.title: Ep 25 · Ch 8 — The Four Givens and the Existential Vacuum
item.description:
Chapter 8: Experiential Therapies
Existential therapy's four givens (death, freedom, isolation, meaninglessness) and the three levels of being — umwelt, mitwelt, eigenwelt — give you a map for locating where a client is actually stuck. Learn why a high-functioning client's inexplicable dread is often an empty eigenwelt, not an attachment injury, and why medicating or reframing that dread strips away the crisis that could produce authenticity.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 8: Experiential Therapies
Existential therapy's four givens (death, freedom, isolation, meaninglessness) and the three levels of being — umwelt, mitwelt, eigenwelt — give you a map for locating where a client is actually stuck. Learn why a high-functioning client's inexplicable dread is often an empty eigenwelt, not an attachment injury, and why medicating or reframing that dread strips away the crisis that could produce authenticity.

item.title: Ep 26 · Ch 8 — Reading the Emotional Compass Beneath Reactive Anger
item.description:
Chapter 8: Experiential Therapies
How emotion-focused therapy treats emotion as adaptive information organized into emotion schemes, and how to distinguish primary from secondary and instrumental emotions in the room. You get the concrete move for bypassing a partner's rage to reach the fear underneath, plus why teaching anger management to a raging client means treating the armor instead of the wound.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 8: Experiential Therapies
How emotion-focused therapy treats emotion as adaptive information organized into emotion schemes, and how to distinguish primary from secondary and instrumental emotions in the room. You get the concrete move for bypassing a partner's rage to reach the fear underneath, plus why teaching anger management to a raging client means treating the armor instead of the wound.

item.title: Ep 27 · Ch 8 — Boundary Disturbances and the Two-Chair Experiment
item.description:
Chapter 8: Experiential Therapies
Gestalt's account of distress as lost contact — introjection, projection, retroflection — and how experiments force a client out of intellectual reporting into present-moment experience. Learn to run a two-chair dialogue for an internal split, interrupt the client who talks about a chair instead of to it, and use body incongruence as your entry point.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 8: Experiential Therapies
Gestalt's account of distress as lost contact — introjection, projection, retroflection — and how experiments force a client out of intellectual reporting into present-moment experience. Learn to run a two-chair dialogue for an internal split, interrupt the client who talks about a chair instead of to it, and use body incongruence as your entry point.

item.title: Ep 28 · Ch 8 — Created Meaning, Discovered Meaning, and Emotion's Limits
item.description:
Chapter 8: Experiential Therapies
A Christian critique of experiential therapy's two core assumptions: that meaning must be invented and that the deepest emotion is always a reliable guide. Learn how to pivot a client from the crushing burden of authoring their own meaning toward discovering it, and how to fully validate a destructive emotion while still holding the moral boundary on the action.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 8: Experiential Therapies
A Christian critique of experiential therapy's two core assumptions: that meaning must be invented and that the deepest emotion is always a reliable guide. Learn how to pivot a client from the crushing burden of authoring their own meaning toward discovering it, and how to fully validate a destructive emotion while still holding the moral boundary on the action.

item.title: Ep 29 · Ch 9 — Why the Identified Patient Keeps the Family Balanced
item.description:
Chapter 9: Family Systems Theory and Therapy
Reframes symptoms as systemic regulation rather than individual pathology, using homeostasis, feedback loops, and the shift from linear to circular causality. Learn to track the loop until it closes and to ask what the family would lose if the symptom vanished.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 9: Family Systems Theory and Therapy
Reframes symptoms as systemic regulation rather than individual pathology, using homeostasis, feedback loops, and the shift from linear to circular causality. Learn to track the loop until it closes and to ask what the family would lose if the symptom vanished.

item.title: Ep 30 · Ch 9 — Mapping Boundaries and Forcing Enactments With Minuchin
item.description:
Chapter 9: Family Systems Theory and Therapy
Walks through structural family therapy: joining the system, mapping subsystems and hierarchy, and staging enactments so the dysfunction plays out live instead of being narrated. Covers unbalancing enmeshed or disengaged boundaries without usurping the parental executive subsystem.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 9: Family Systems Theory and Therapy
Walks through structural family therapy: joining the system, mapping subsystems and hierarchy, and staging enactments so the dysfunction plays out live instead of being narrated. Covers unbalancing enmeshed or disengaged boundaries without usurping the parental executive subsystem.

item.title: Ep 31 · Ch 9 — Triangles, Differentiation, and Staying the Non-Anxious Presence
item.description:
Chapter 9: Family Systems Theory and Therapy
Applies Bowenian theory to high-conflict sessions where emotional flooding makes skills-based work useless: de-triangulating, taking I-positions, and tracing anxiety through the genogram. The core lesson is that the therapist's own regulated nervous system is the primary intervention.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 9: Family Systems Theory and Therapy
Applies Bowenian theory to high-conflict sessions where emotional flooding makes skills-based work useless: de-triangulating, taking I-positions, and tracing anxiety through the genogram. The core lesson is that the therapist's own regulated nervous system is the primary intervention.

item.title: Ep 32 · Ch 9 — Relational Ontology, the Trinity, and Systemic Grace
item.description:
Chapter 9: Family Systems Theory and Therapy
Makes the case that the unit of analysis is the relationship, not the individual, and connects that claim to a Christian view of persons as ontologically relational. Shows how circular causality lifts the scapegoat's burden and dissolves blame into shared responsibility.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 9: Family Systems Theory and Therapy
Makes the case that the unit of analysis is the relationship, not the individual, and connects that claim to a Christian view of persons as ontologically relational. Shows how circular causality lifts the scapegoat's burden and dissolves blame into shared responsibility.

item.title: Ep 33 · Ch 10 — Mapping the Ecosystem: When Anxiety Is Adaptation
item.description:
Chapter 10: Community Psychology and Preventative Intervention Strategies
Listeners learn to read a client's distress through Bronfenbrenner's nested systems (micro, meso, exo, macro), so that a single mother's "treatment-resistant" anxiety is reframed as an accurate survival response to a violent neighborhood, precarious work, and vanished school supports. The episode also covers the Institute of Medicine's universal, selective, and indicated prevention categories, and why the medical model keeps pulling people out of the river instead of walking upstream.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 10: Community Psychology and Preventative Intervention Strategies
Listeners learn to read a client's distress through Bronfenbrenner's nested systems (micro, meso, exo, macro), so that a single mother's "treatment-resistant" anxiety is reframed as an accurate survival response to a violent neighborhood, precarious work, and vanished school supports. The episode also covers the Institute of Medicine's universal, selective, and indicated prevention categories, and why the medical model keeps pulling people out of the river instead of walking upstream.

item.title: Ep 34 · Ch 10 — Hunting for Assets Instead of Diagnosing Deficits
item.description:
Chapter 10: Community Psychology and Preventative Intervention Strategies
A practical playbook for inverting the therapist's deficit-detective habit: conduct an asset map of personal, social, and spiritual resources, build an asset genogram that charts family resilience alongside family damage, and match coping style to whether the stressor is controllable. It also names the "White Knight" trap — your fifty minutes cannot outcompete the client's other 167 hours, so the goal is to empower their natural ecosystem, not replace it.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 10: Community Psychology and Preventative Intervention Strategies
A practical playbook for inverting the therapist's deficit-detective habit: conduct an asset map of personal, social, and spiritual resources, build an asset genogram that charts family resilience alongside family damage, and match coping style to whether the stressor is controllable. It also names the "White Knight" trap — your fifty minutes cannot outcompete the client's other 167 hours, so the goal is to empower their natural ecosystem, not replace it.

item.title: Ep 35 · Ch 10 — Shalom as the Ground of Clinical Advocacy
item.description:
Chapter 10: Community Psychology and Preventative Intervention Strategies
Explores why secular community psychology's case for social justice rests on shaky foundations, and how the biblical vision of shalom — peace with God, self, others, and creation — gives Christian clinicians a sturdier one. Listeners learn to assess where shalom has ruptured in a case, to treat case management and advocacy as legitimate clinical acts, and to avoid both hyper-spiritualizing suffering and collapsing into relativism about culture.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 10: Community Psychology and Preventative Intervention Strategies
Explores why secular community psychology's case for social justice rests on shaky foundations, and how the biblical vision of shalom — peace with God, self, others, and creation — gives Christian clinicians a sturdier one. Listeners learn to assess where shalom has ruptured in a case, to treat case management and advocacy as legitimate clinical acts, and to avoid both hyper-spiritualizing suffering and collapsing into relativism about culture.

item.title: Ep 36 · Ch 11 — Four Disciplined Ways to Borrow Across Models
item.description:
Chapter 11: Responsible Eclecticism and the Challenges of Contemporary Practice
Distinguishes technical eclecticism, theoretical integration, assimilative integration, and common factors as four different answers to drawing on multiple therapies, and shows why the "supermarket" approach leaves clients feeling a therapist has no rudder. Listeners learn to keep a theoretical home base while borrowing a grounding or soothing intervention when a client's nervous system goes offline mid-protocol.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 11: Responsible Eclecticism and the Challenges of Contemporary Practice
Distinguishes technical eclecticism, theoretical integration, assimilative integration, and common factors as four different answers to drawing on multiple therapies, and shows why the "supermarket" approach leaves clients feeling a therapist has no rudder. Listeners learn to keep a theoretical home base while borrowing a grounding or soothing intervention when a client's nervous system goes offline mid-protocol.

item.title: Ep 37 · Ch 11 — Technique Is Only Fifteen Percent of Change
item.description:
Chapter 11: Responsible Eclecticism and the Challenges of Contemporary Practice
Breaks down the pie chart of therapeutic change — 40% extratherapeutic context, 30% alliance, 15% hope, 15% technique — and argues that chasing the next intervention means chasing the smallest slice. Listeners get concrete moves for the other 85%: alliance check-ins, auditing the client's 167 hours outside the room, and explicitly priming expectancy in session one.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 11: Responsible Eclecticism and the Challenges of Contemporary Practice
Breaks down the pie chart of therapeutic change — 40% extratherapeutic context, 30% alliance, 15% hope, 15% technique — and argues that chasing the next intervention means chasing the smallest slice. Listeners get concrete moves for the other 85%: alliance check-ins, auditing the client's 167 hours outside the room, and explicitly priming expectancy in session one.

item.title: Ep 38 · Ch 12 — Which Divine Office Does This Client Need?
item.description:
Chapter 12: Christian Psychotherapy and the Person of the Christian Psychotherapist
Reframes the therapist's presence as incarnational rather than technical, mapping four "offices" of God — Paraclete, Reconciler, Healer, Wise Counselor — onto specific client needs. Listeners learn how retreating into the Office of the Expert stalls combative clients, why clinical "neutrality" fails the abused, and how the wounded-healer check keeps you from imaging your own unhealed pain.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 12: Christian Psychotherapy and the Person of the Christian Psychotherapist
Reframes the therapist's presence as incarnational rather than technical, mapping four "offices" of God — Paraclete, Reconciler, Healer, Wise Counselor — onto specific client needs. Listeners learn how retreating into the Office of the Expert stalls combative clients, why clinical "neutrality" fails the abused, and how the wounded-healer check keeps you from imaging your own unhealed pain.

item.title: Ep 39 · Ch 12 — Character Over Compliance: Ten Virtues for Clinicians
item.description:
Chapter 12: Christian Psychotherapy and the Person of the Christian Psychotherapist
Argues that ethics codes set a floor for behavior but no ceiling for character, then walks the ten virtue contrasts (compassion vs. elitism, community vs. isolation, agape vs. mere unconditional positive regard) that define Christian clinical practice. Listeners get concrete practice steps: an accountability audit, a caseload elitism check, and a transparency move for owning mistakes in session.
item.itunes:author: Modern Psychotherapies Discussion
item.itunes:summary:
Chapter 12: Christian Psychotherapy and the Person of the Christian Psychotherapist
Argues that ethics codes set a floor for behavior but no ceiling for character, then walks the ten virtue contrasts (compassion vs. elitism, community vs. isolation, agape vs. mere unconditional positive regard) that define Christian clinical practice. Listeners get concrete practice steps: an accountability audit, a caseload elitism check, and a transparency move for owning mistakes in session.
